pitfalls: Tier-based rate limits are aggressively enforced and shared across concurrent threads
Streaming connections can drop silently after ~60s of inactivity
mitigations: Implement token-bucket rate limiting locally and partition API keys across worker pools
Add application-level keep-alive pings or timeouts for long-running streams

pitfalls: Overloaded capacity during peak hours leading to 529 errors
Strict context window validation that fails before processing begins
mitigations: Use exponential backoff with jitter specifically for 529 and 429 status codes
Pre-calculate token counts locally to avoid validation errors wasting API calls

pitfalls: Safety filters can silently block outputs without clear error codes
Frequent 503 errors during model updates or regional failovers
mitigations: Implement prompt sanitization to reduce false-positive safety triggers
Build regional fallback routing to different Google Cloud endpoints

pitfalls: Rapid scaling has led to intermittent DNS resolution issues
Endpoint inconsistency between their self-hosted and cloud offerings
mitigations: Pin the IP addresses of Mistral endpoints in your DNS cache
Use distinct configuration blocks for Mistral Cloud vs. Azure-hosted Mistral

pitfalls: Extremely aggressive rate limits on free tier; easy to hit concurrent request caps
Hardware utilization spikes cause sudden request failures when capacity is full
mitigations: Implement strict queuing logic rather than direct concurrent API blasts
Have a fallback to a slower LLM provider when Groq returns 429

pitfalls: Cold start times on less popular models can exceed standard timeout thresholds
Inconsistent latency spikes during US business hours
mitigations: Set HTTP client timeouts to at least 120 seconds for specific models
Use their dedicated endpoint feature for production workloads

pitfalls: Strict prompt truncation rules that silently alter inputs
Connectivity drops between their load balancers and backend workers
mitigations: Log input token lengths to detect truncation before it affects outputs
Use connection pooling with retry logic on connection resets

pitfalls: Rate limits fluctuate based on overall cluster load
Some open-source models have unexpected stop tokens causing truncated outputs
mitigations: Monitor response finish_reason to catch unexpected length truncations
Implement adaptive rate limiting based on 429 response headers

pitfalls: Less infrastructure redundancy than top-tier providers
Occasional CUDA out-of-memory errors bubbled up as 500s
mitigations: Do not use for critical unattended workflows without a fallback
Parse error messages for OOM hints and reduce batch sizes dynamically

pitfalls: High latency due to real-time web search grounding
Search index can temporarily fail, causing the whole request to fail
mitigations: Set high timeout thresholds (30s+)
Separate Perplexity calls from synchronous user-facing loops

pitfalls: Search depth parameters drastically increase timeout likelihood
Occasional empty results returned as a 200 OK, breaking agent logic
mitigations: Default to 'basic' search depth unless 'advanced' is strictly necessary
Always validate that the returned array length is > 0 after a successful call

pitfalls: Google blocks SerpAPI's underlying infrastructure periodically, causing upstream failures
Pricing can spiral out of control if agents loop search calls
mitigations: Implement strict caching of search results locally
Catch 'Google internal error' upstream messages and retry after a long backoff

pitfalls: 100 queries/day limit on free tier is extremely easy for agents to exhaust
Strict quota limits that do not reset until midnight Pacific Time
mitigations: Aggressively cache results with semantic similarity checks before querying
Set up alerting at 80% quota utilization

pitfalls: Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key header errors are common when keys are rotated
Market parameter defaults can skew results unexpectedly
mitigations: Abstract authentication to handle Azure key rotation seamlessly
Explicitly set the 'cc' and 'setLang' parameters in every request

pitfalls: Index size is smaller than Google/Bing, leading to empty results for niche queries
Rate limit headers can be inconsistent across edge nodes
mitigations: Use Brave as a primary with Google as a fallback for empty sets
Implement distributed rate limiting rather than relying purely on their headers

pitfalls: Neural search is computationally heavy and prone to timeouts
Content filtering can silently drop highly relevant results
mitigations: Use 'auto' prompt parameter carefully, prefer explicit keyword-based sub-queries
Set specific timeouts to 15s and failover to a keyword search API

pitfalls: Frequent backend changes break backward compatibility
Less reliable during high-traffic global news events
mitigations: Pin to a specific API version in your headers if possible
Treat as a secondary/triary search source due to instability

pitfalls: Slow response times due to rendering JavaScript on the fly
Occasionally returns HTML instead of parsed JSON when parsers break
mitigations: Always use asynchronous polling endpoints instead of synchronous waits
Add defensive parsing to handle raw HTML responses gracefully

pitfalls: Idempotency keys required but sometimes silently ignored if formatted incorrectly
Webhook delivery can be heavily delayed during massive sales events like Black Friday
mitigations: Generate UUIDv4 idempotency keys and store them in your DB mapped to the operation
Implement Stripe's webhook signature verification and an exponential backoff replay queue

pitfalls: OAuth token generation fails more often than it should, requiring constant refreshes
Sandbox environment behavior frequently diverges from production
mitigations: Cache OAuth tokens and refresh them proactively 60 seconds before expiry
Run integration tests against the live environment with very low dollar amounts

pitfalls: Location IDs are heavily coupled to requests; omitting them causes cryptic errors
Strict rate limits on tokenization endpoints
mitigations: Store location IDs in environment configuration, never hardcode them
Implement a local card tokenization cache if processing batches

pitfalls: API is often treated as a secondary product to their UI, leading to missing features
Webhooks can arrive out of order or duplicate during high load
mitigations: Make your systems idempotent based on the 'event_id' in webhooks
Do not rely on their API for real-time inventory syncing

pitfalls: Uses a legacy API architecture (v2) that requires strange parameter encoding
Auth signatures require exact ordering of parameters, failing if sorted incorrectly
mitigations: Use a dedicated, well-tested SDK rather than raw HTTP calls
Write strict unit tests for their specific signature generation algorithm

pitfalls: Basic auth with base64 can be tricky to get right in some serverless environments
Webhook IPs are dynamic and hard to whitelist
mitigations: Double-check base64 encoding padding in your HTTP client
Verify webhook signatures cryptographically instead of relying on IP whitelisting

pitfalls: Error responses are deeply nested and require complex parsing to extract user-facing messages
Test environment occasionally has trouble simulating specific bank declines
mitigations: Build a robust error-mapping layer to translate Adyen error codes to generic UI states
Maintain a mock service for edge-case bank response testing

pitfalls: Strict unsubscribe and spam scoring blocks legitimate emails if custom args are missing
Dynamic template IDs can break silently if a newer version is published and the old one is archived
mitigations: Pin specific template versions rather than using 'latest' or 'active'
Pre-validate email addresses with SendGrid's single sender verification API

pitfalls: DNS propagation issues can cause temporary 404s on custom tracking domains
Rate limits are applied per-IP, which breaks in auto-scaling environments
mitigations: Use a static outbound IP or an elastic IP for your email-sending servers
Implement a local DNS validation check before sending via new domains

pitfalls: Strict domain authentication requirements; unverified domains fail silently
Newer API, occasionally missing features that legacy providers have (like complex batch routing)
mitigations: Automate domain verification via their API during infrastructure setup
Wrap in an abstraction layer to easily swap out if missing features block you

pitfalls: Extremely strict content rules; transactional emails cannot look remotely like marketing
Message streams are segregated, and sending to the wrong stream drops the email
mitigations: Explicitly map your application contexts to the correct Postmark Message Stream ID
Pre-process HTML templates to strip out marketing-like styling

pitfalls: Sandbox mode restricts everything until manually lifted, catching new devs off guard
IAM policies for SES are notoriously complex and easy to misconfigure
mitigations: Automate sandbox exit requests via AWS Support API if possible, or document the manual step clearly
Use AWS SES-specific IAM condition keys to lock down sending permissions

pitfalls: Tightly coupled to main Mailchimp infrastructure; Mailchimp outages affect Mandrill
Legacy API structure feels dated and returns XML-like error strings sometimes
mitigations: If using Mailchimp for marketing too, ensure your marketing automations aren't blocking transactional queues
Abstract the API response format into a standard internal DTO

pitfalls: Smaller provider, so IP reputation warming can take longer
Data variables must exactly match the template schema or it 400s
mitigations: Run a low-volume warmup campaign before switching primary transactional traffic
Fetch the template schema dynamically via their API before constructing payloads

pitfalls: Rate limits are per-app-per-workspace, but retry headers can be misleading
Message payloads exceeding 3000 characters in text fields fail silently by truncating
mitigations: Implement token rotation across multiple app instances if hitting workspace limits
Pre-truncate strings to 2999 characters before posting

pitfalls: Highly aggressive rate limits on message endpoints (5/5s globally per bot)
File uploads require specific multipart/form-data formatting that breaks in some SDKs
mitigations: Use a strict global queue for Discord API calls, do not rely on concurrent execution
Always use well-maintained official or highly-starred libraries for file uploads

pitfalls: getUpdates long-polling can silently die, causing the bot to stop receiving messages
Sending messages to users who haven't started a conversation throws forbidden errors
mitigations: Prefer Webhooks over getUpdates for production reliability
Wrap sends in a try-catch that handles 'blocked by user' gracefully without alerting

pitfalls: US carrier filtering is aggressive and rejects messages that look like spam
Billing outages can cause messages to queue for hours without failing
mitigations: Register all sending numbers with US A2P 10DLC immediately
Monitor queue sizes via the API and alert if pending messages exceed thresholds

pitfalls: Template approvals are arbitrarily rejected by Meta
24-hour messaging window is strictly enforced and hard to track across stateless systems
mitigations: Keep a precise timestamp of the last user message in your DB to check window validity
Avoid dynamic variables in templates that Meta's AI might flag as promotional

pitfalls: Deduplication keys must be perfectly managed or you'll flood on-call engineers
REST API is eventually consistent; pushing an alert might not show in the UI immediately
mitigations: Use highly deterministic dedup keys (e.g., alert_id + check_name)
Do not immediately query the REST API after a POST to verify state

pitfalls: Routing rules are evaluated server-side; a misconfiguration silently drops the alert
API keys are specific to teams/regions, causing 403s if used globally
mitigations: Set up a 'black hole' integration to catch alerts that fail routing rules
Implement a key management system that maps teams to specific Opsgenie API keys

pitfalls: Eventually consistent listings after overwrites can confuse agents expecting immediate reads
Request signing (SigV4) is complex and breaks if the system clock is slightly skewed
mitigations: Use strong read-after-write consistency where available, or add a 1s delay
Run NTP synchronization strictly and use AWS SDKs to handle signing

pitfalls: Default service accounts lack storage permissions, causing silent auth failures
Resumable uploads require careful state management of the upload session URIs
mitigations: Explicitly bind the 'roles/storage.objectAdmin' role to service accounts
Persist resumable upload URIs to a database so interrupted uploads can be resumed

pitfalls: SAS tokens have very specific formatting rules (e.g., query string ordering) that fail if malformed
Soft delete feature means deleted files still incur storage costs and block re-uploads
mitigations: Use managed identities instead of SAS tokens for server-to-server communication
Implement a hard purge script that bypasses soft delete for temporary agent files

pitfalls: S3 compatibility layer is not 100% complete; some multipart upload calls differ
Lifecycle rules have a delay before taking effect, causing buckets to fill up temporarily
mitigations: Test specific SDK features against R2 before assuming S3 parity
Set up external bucket size monitoring instead of relying solely on lifecycle rules

pitfalls: Upload authorization tokens expire after 24 hours, requiring a two-step upload process
Less global edge presence means higher latency outside North America
mitigations: Cache the upload URL and auth token locally with an expiry check
Use Cloudflare as a caching proxy in front of B2 for global reads

pitfalls: Strict 90-day minimum storage deletion policy results in unexpected billing if objects are overwritten/deleted early
Occasional network-level drops between their data centers and AWS/Azure transit
mitigations: Abstract the storage layer so temporary files go to local disk or another provider
Use connection retry logic with shorter TCP timeouts

pitfalls: Free tier pauses databases after 1 week of inactivity, causing hard connection refused errors
PostgREST layer can choke on highly complex nested JSON queries
mitigations: Implement a keep-alive pinger on free-tier projects
Pre-aggregate complex joins in database views and query the views via the REST API

pitfalls: Offline behavior in server-side environments can lead to race conditions when coming back online
Cascading security rule evaluations can timeout on deeply nested structures
mitigations: Disable offline persistence in server-side SDK configurations
Flatten data structures to avoid deep nesting in security rules

pitfalls: Foreign key constraints are disabled by default, silently allowing bad data
Schema migrations require branching and merging which can confuse automated deployment pipelines
mitigations: Enforce foreign keys at the application layer if using PlanetScale
Automate PlanetScale schema migrations via their API tightly coupled to CI/CD

pitfalls: Serverless scale-to-zero causes cold starts that drop initial TCP connections
Connection pooling is required but PgBouncer can mangle prepared statements
mitigations: Use their serverless driver which handles websockets and scaling natively
Do not use prepared statements when connecting through an external pooler

pitfalls: Embedded replicas (libSQL) can fall out of sync with the primary, leading to stale reads
Row-level locking behavior differs from standard SQLite in distributed contexts
mitigations: Use the 'sync' API endpoint explicitly before performing critical reads on replicas
Design schemas to minimize the need for cross-node locks

pitfalls: Connection strings change when clusters scale or update, breaking hardcoded configs
Serverless instances have extremely low concurrent connection limits
mitigations: Always use the SRV connection string format, never the standard TCP format
Use MongoDB's serverless driver or heavy connection pooling

pitfalls: Pipelining is heavily restricted over HTTP, forcing sequential REST calls for bulk ops
Data eviction policies can catch developers off guard if max memory is reached
mitigations: Use their custom SDK that handles pipelining over HTTP automatically
Set strict alerts at 80% memory utilization to prevent uncontrolled evictions

pitfalls: FQL query language has a steep learning curve and is easy to DDoS your own database with bad queries
Abort transactions frequently under high contention, requiring complex retry logic
mitigations: Implement query cost analysis in staging before deploying complex FQL to production
Use exponential backoff specifically tailored for Fauna's 4xx transaction aborts

pitfalls: Index scaling operations can cause hours of degraded performance or timeouts
Pod-based billing can spike unexpectedly if upserts exceed memory limits and trigger auto-scaling
mitigations: Over-provision index dimensions and pods initially to avoid hot-scaling
Batch upserts strictly during off-peak hours

pitfalls: Snapshot creation locks the cluster, causing write timeouts if triggered during high load
gRPC connection setup is required for optimal performance, which some HTTP clients handle poorly
mitigations: Schedule snapshots exclusively during maintenance windows
Use the official gRPC client instead of REST for production vector operations

pitfalls: GraphQL queries can cause Out-Of-Memory (OOM) crashes if filters are too broad
Module initialization (like reranker) adds significant overhead to startup and query times
mitigations: Strictly enforce pagination limits in GraphQL queries
Disable unused modules to reduce memory footprint and query latency

pitfalls: Managed service is relatively new and experiences frequent restarts
In-memory nature of default configurations leads to data loss if the pod crashes
mitigations: Run Chroma self-hosted with a persistent volume for critical applications
If using managed, implement a fallback to a local SQLite-backed Chroma instance

pitfalls: Complex indexing strategies are difficult to configure correctly via API
Data import APIs are asynchronous and fail silently if CSV/JSON schemas mismatch
mitigations: Pre-validate data schemas against the collection schema before calling the bulk import API
Poll the import task status endpoint until completion before querying

pitfalls: Heavy background processing for memory extraction causes slow API response times
Occasional memory graph corruption during concurrent agent writes to the same session
mitigations: Fire-and-forget memory writes when possible, don't block the agent on Zep
Use unique session IDs per agent thread to avoid concurrent write locks

pitfalls: Schema updates require an explicit API call to re-index, which drops queries momentarily
Typo-tolerance feature can drastically increase CPU usage and latency on large datasets
mitigations: Apply schema updates during planned downtime or with application-level graceful degradation
Monitor CPU metrics and disable typo-tolerance if latency degrades

pitfalls: Document updates are processed asynchronously; querying immediately returns stale data
Heavy filtering on large datasets bypasses indices and scans the whole database, causing timeouts
mitigations: Wait for the 'processed' task status before assuming data is live
Pre-filter data into smaller sub-indexes rather than relying on runtime filtering

pitfalls: JavaScript rendering is slow and frequently fails on highly dynamic SPAs
Pricing model makes it expensive to use for broad web crawling
mitigations: Set high timeouts (30s+) and use the synchronous endpoint only for critical single-page fetches
Pre-filter URLs using a fast HEAD request checker before sending to Firecrawl

pitfalls: Concurrent session limits are strictly enforced and drop connections without warning
Memory leaks in user-provided JS functions can crash the shared browser instance
mitigations: Wrap browserless calls in a local semaphore to strictly cap concurrency
Sandbox user JS functions and always include try/catch/finally blocks to close pages

pitfalls: Premium proxies increase latency drastically
Some anti-bot systems (like Cloudflare Turnstile) still block their infrastructure
mitigations: Use standard scraping first, fall back to premium proxies only on 403s
Combine with a local CAPTCHA solver if hitting advanced bot protection

pitfalls: Actor execution is asynchronous; polling for results adds complexity and latency
Shared compute resources mean your actor might queue behind a massive crawl job
mitigations: Allocate dedicated compute for production scraping actors
Use webhooks to push results to your API instead of constant polling

pitfalls: Anti-bot bypass success rate fluctuates daily as providers update their models
Response bodies occasionally return corrupted encoding for non-UTF-8 sites
mitigations: Implement a fallback chain: ScrapeOps -> Firecrawl -> Local Playwright
Always force response encoding to UTF-8 and strip invalid byte sequences

pitfalls: Auto-rotate feature can sometimes loop through dead proxies, eating up credits
Data extraction AI sometimes hallucinates JSON structures that don't match the requested schema
mitigations: Disable auto-rotate and manually select proxy types for specific domains
Always wrap ZenRows extraction in a JSON Schema validator

pitfalls: High failure rate on modern React/Vue sites if JavaScript rendering isn't explicitly requested
Can return 200 OK with a CAPTCHA page HTML body instead of actual content
mitigations: Always pass 'render=true' for known SPA domains
Pre-scan the HTML body for CAPTCHA indicators before passing to parsing logic

pitfalls: Enterprise pricing makes it prohibitive for high-volume agent testing
Superproxy routing can sometimes geolocate to the wrong region
mitigations: Explicitly pass the 'geo_location' parameter in every request
Use Oxylabs strictly as a last-resort fallback due to cost

pitfalls: Extremely strict content moderation filters reject benign prompts frequently
Generation times can spike unpredictably based on cluster load
mitigations: Implement robust prompt sanitization to remove ambiguous phrasing
Use asynchronous polling patterns for generation to prevent HTTP timeouts

pitfalls: Frequent backend infrastructure changes breaking API clients
Credit consumption calculation is opaque and can drain accounts fast on retries
mitigations: Pin your API client to specific engine versions, do not use 'latest'
Log credits remaining before and after generation calls to detect anomalies

pitfalls: Cold starts on GPU machines can take 30-60 seconds
Webhook delivery is unreliable, making polling strictly necessary
mitigations: Always poll the API using an exponential backoff, do not rely on webhooks
Keep a dummy prediction running periodically to keep the GPU warm if cost allows

pitfalls: Relies on reverse-engineering Discord's websocket and REST APIs, breaks constantly
Account bans are common if usage patterns look automated
mitigations: Do not use for production applications under any circumstances
If experimenting, throttle requests to mimic human speed and add random delays

pitfalls: Character limits are strictly enforced and easily exceeded by chatty agents
Voice cloning requires manual approval which blocks fully automated workflows
mitigations: Pre-truncate agent text to strict character limits before sending to API
Stick to pre-made voices for autonomous agent systems

pitfalls: Video generation takes minutes and will break standard HTTP timeouts
API is frequently throttled in favor of their web UI users
mitigations: Use exclusively with asynchronous task queues
Check status pages before initiating long video render queues

pitfalls: API is largely undocumented and reverse-engineered from their web client
Generation queues are heavily rate-limited, returning silent failures
mitigations: Treat as experimental and build heavy fallbacks
Implement aggressive local rate limiting to avoid triggering their shadow bans

pitfalls: V2 API frequently changes required payload structures
Audio chunking for streaming can result in glitchy artifacts if network blips occur
mitigations: Use their v1 API if v2 continues to shift under you
Download the final generated file rather than relying on streaming for archival

pitfalls: Secondary rate limits trigger even if primary rate limits aren't hit
Webhook payloads can arrive out of order during massive events (like GitHub Saturation day)
mitigations: Use conditional requests (If-None-Match) to avoid hitting secondary limits
Process webhooks idempotently by sorting them by the 'X-GitHub-Delivery' header timestamp

pitfalls: Pagination is complex (Link headers vs offset) and inconsistent across endpoints
API response size for file trees can be massive and cause OOM in weak clients
mitigations: Use a robust GitLab API client that handles Link header pagination natively
Explicitly request only needed fields using the 'select' parameter

pitfalls: Build logs API streams can hang indefinitely if the build process zombie-stalls
Git integration webhooks can trigger duplicate deployments if not deduplicated
mitigations: Add a hard timeout to build log streaming connections
Check the 'sha' header on push webhooks and ignore if the latest deployment already matches

pitfalls: Deploy hook URLs are static and easily discoverable, leading to accidental triggers
Form submission API has strict spam filtering that can drop legitimate bot submissions
mitigations: Obfuscate deploy hook URLs or trigger deployments via Git instead
Add a custom header to form submissions from your agent and whitelist it in Netlify settings

pitfalls: GraphQL API is frequently changed or deprecated without much notice
Infrastructure provisioning can fail silently, leaving services in a 'crashing' loop
mitigations: Watch their GitHub repository for schema changes
Implement health check polling immediately after provisioning to catch silent failures

pitfalls: Free tier services spin down, causing 502s that agents might misinterpret as app errors
Deployments can hang in 'live' state while the underlying container is actually crashing
mitigations: Never use free tier for agent backend dependencies
Verify deployment health via the service logs endpoint, not just the status endpoint

pitfalls: Machines API requires complex JSON-to-TOML-to-JSON mental models for configuration
Frequent networking hiccups between their internal edge proxies and your app container
mitigations: Manage fly.toml files in Git and push via CLI rather than constructing API payloads
Implement aggressive TCP keep-alives for internal service-to-service communication

pitfalls: API rate limits are incredibly strict for open source projects
Artifact download URLs expire very quickly, making long-running agent tasks fail
mitigations: Cache artifacts in your own S3 bucket if needed for long-running processes
Use a premium API token if exceeding 1000 requests/hour

pitfalls: Platform has degraded significantly in reliability over the years
Migration from .org to .com causes widespread auth failures
mitigations: Migrate to GitHub Actions if possible
If forced to use, implement heavy retry logic with 5-minute backoffs

pitfalls: Rate limits are based on a complex 'app usage' tier system, not just raw requests
Custom property schema changes take time to propagate, causing 400s immediately after creation
mitigations: Dynamically read the 'X-HubSpot-RateLimit-Remaining' headers and pause execution before hitting 0
Wait 5 seconds after creating a custom property before trying to write to it

pitfalls: Session tokens expire rapidly and JWT flows are painfully complex to implement
SOQL queries are prone to governor limits that crash the agent if querying too many rows
mitigations: Use Salesforce Connected App OAuth flows with automatic token refresh
Implement pagination and strictly limit SOQL row counts using LIMIT clauses

pitfalls: API is incredibly slow for reading large databases due to lack of server-side filtering
Rich text block creation requires deeply nested JSON that is easy to get wrong
mitigations: Fetch database schemas once and cache them locally, only sync deltas
Build a custom abstraction layer that flattens nested block structures for LLM context

pitfalls: 5 requests per second limit is brutally low for agent loops
Webhooks for table changes can be delayed by minutes
mitigations: Implement an aggressive local queue that batches writes and drains at 4 req/s
Poll for changes if latency is critical, as webhooks cannot be trusted for speed

pitfalls: GraphQL complexity limits are strictly enforced, blocking deep nested queries
Real-time webhooks require setting up a public endpoint and handling signature verification perfectly
mitigations: Flatten GraphQL queries and avoid deep nesting
Use their SDK to handle webhook signature verification rather than writing custom crypto

pitfalls: Issue payload structures differ wildly between Jira Cloud and Jira Server
Custom fields require internal IDs (customfield_10043) instead of names, making dynamic querying a nightmare
mitigations: Create a mapping cache of custom field names to IDs on startup
Abstract the API behind a generic 'create issue' function that handles the custom field injection

pitfalls: Optimistic locking requires handling '412 Precondition Failed' constantly in fast agents
Tags and projects must exist before being assigned to a task, requiring multiple round trips
mitigations: Implement a local state cache to avoid 412 conflicts when possible
Use 'sync' API endpoints to batch-read task states rather than individual GETs

pitfalls: Board visibility changes can silently break API tokens if not scoped correctly
File attachments require a two-step process (upload to Trello, then attach) that frequently fails
mitigations: Use API tokens generated with 'read/write' scope explicitly tied to the board
Skip Trello attachments and host files on S3, linking them in the card description

pitfalls: OAuth token refresh flow is flaky and often returns invalid tokens
Email matching logic is overly aggressive and can merge unrelated leads
mitigations: Implement a fallback to Google OAuth directly if Copper's wrapper fails
Always provide explicit lead IDs rather than relying on email matching for updates

pitfalls: Billing is extremely aggressive; a buggy agent loop can cost hundreds of dollars overnight
ZERO_RESULTS is returned as a 200 OK, which lazy error handling misses
mitigations: Implement hard daily caps on the API key via Google Cloud Console
Explicitly check for 'status' == 'ZERO_RESULTS' in the JSON response

pitfalls: Forward geocoding heavily prioritizes known places, making obscure address lookup fail
Permanent account suspension if rate limits are exceeded by more than 10%
mitigations: Use structured input (address components) rather than single strings for better results
Set strict client-side rate limiters to avoid catastrophic account bans

pitfalls: API key rotation invalidates existing SDK instances if not handled
Complex API tiers make it hard to predict when you hit paid limits
mitigations: Abstract API key injection so rotation doesn't require app restarts
Monitor the specific API metrics endpoint to track usage against tier limits

pitfalls: Less accurate data in non-European regions
Batch geocoding API has a different authentication mechanism than single geocoding
mitigations: Use Mapbox/Google for North America, TomTom for Europe
Maintain separate credential configurations for batch vs. single endpoints

pitfalls: Strictly requires a valid User-Agent header, will block you if you use a default HTTP client string
1 request per second limit is brutally enforced; you will get IP banned otherwise
mitigations: Set a custom User-Agent including your app name and email
Implement a 1.1-second sleep between requests via a local queue

pitfalls: Management API token generation is rate limited and slow, shouldn't be done synchronously
Custom database connection scripts can timeout, locking users out of login
mitigations: Cache Management API tokens aggressively
Keep custom login scripts extremely lightweight; offload logic to webhooks post-login

pitfalls: Frontend SDK and Backend SDK can get out of sync, causing token validation mismatches
Webhook signatures use a specific encoding that trips up standard base64 decoders
mitigations: Use their pre-built middleware to avoid SDK version mismatches
Use their official webhook utility (svix) rather than writing custom base64 logic

pitfalls: Email enumeration protection makes it hard to differentiate between 'wrong password' and 'user doesn't exist'
Admin SDK overrides security rules, which developers often forget when migrating from client SDKs
mitigations: Design UX flows that don't explicitly need to know if an account exists
Strictly audit Admin SDK usage to ensure security rules are maintained

pitfalls: GoTrue (the underlying auth engine) logs are difficult to access and debug
Rate limits on password reset emails can block legitimate users if an agent loops incorrectly
mitigations: Implement frontend error mapping for GoTrue's sometimes cryptic error codes
Hard-limit password reset attempts at the application layer

pitfalls: Lambda triggers attached to Cognito add massive latency to sign-in flows
User pool app client secrets are required for server-side flows, but easy to misconfigure
mitigations: Keep Lambda triggers synchronous and fast (<200ms)
Abstract the SecretHash calculation into a shared utility function

pitfalls: Passwordless magic links require careful routing handling if your app has multiple subdomains
Session tokens have short lifespans, requiring frequent background refreshes
mitigations: Explicitly set the redirect URLs in the Stytch dashboard for all subdomains
Use their backend SDK session middleware to handle transparent token rotation

pitfalls: If you hit rate limits, Sentry drops events silently without telling your app
Envelope format is strict; malformed JSON payloads are rejected without processing
mitigations: Implement client-side sampling to never exceed Sentry's ingestion limits
Validate envelope structure locally before transmitting over the network

pitfalls: Custom metrics payload limits can cause entire batches to be rejected if one metric is malformed
High cardinality metrics will be dropped server-side, hiding real issues
mitigations: Pre-validate metric names and tags against Datadog's strict character requirements
Implement aggressive aggregation at the agent level to avoid high cardinality

pitfalls: Batching is required for high throughput, but oversized batches fail
Event properties exceeding 100KB are silently truncated, breaking analytics queries
mitigations: Cap event payload sizes at the application layer (e.g., 50KB)
Use their official SDKs which handle batching and flushing automatically

pitfalls: Strict data schema enforcement rejects events with unexpected properties
If your distinct_id changes for a user, their history is fragmented
mitigations: Use Mixpanel's 'profile update' endpoints to alias IDs before sending events
Strictly type-check events against a schema before sending

pitfalls: Prometheus remote write limits can cause metrics to be rejected during traffic spikes
API tokens have complex permission scopes that are easy to misconfigure
mitigations: Use a local Prometheus instance to buffer and batch remote writes
Create a dedicated 'agent-metrics' API key with minimal permissions

pitfalls: NRQL queries can timeout if they scan too much data, returning 504s
API query keys have different rate limits than ingest keys, confusing developers
mitigations: Always include time bounds (SINCE/UNTIL) in NRQL queries to limit data scans
Document and separate API query keys from ingest keys in your secrets manager

pitfalls: Line protocol syntax errors reject the entire batch, not just the bad line
Cardinality limits on tags will silently drop data when exceeded
mitigations: Parse line protocol locally before sending to ensure exact formatting
Monitor cardinality dashboards and alert before hitting the 90% threshold

pitfalls: Querying too far back in time causes OOM crashes on the Prometheus server
No built-in authentication; exposing it to agents requires a strict reverse proxy setup
mitigations: Enforce 'max_source_interval' limits in your reverse proxy query params
Put Prometheus behind an OAuth proxy and pass tokens via header

pitfalls: Leaky bucket algorithm for rate limits means sudden bursts heavily throttle subsequent requests
GraphQL API is heavily favored; REST API is slowly being deprecated for complex queries
mitigations: Implement a leaky bucket algorithm locally that mirrors Shopify's exact parameters
Migrate agent integrations to the Shopify GraphQL API

pitfalls: Performance degrades linearly as product database size grows due to WordPress postmeta table
Authentication via Application Passwords can be stripped by aggressive security plugins
mitigations: Implement aggressive Redis caching for WooCommerce read endpoints
Whitelist the agent's IP explicitly in security plugins

pitfalls: API is highly modular, meaning endpoints might return 404 if a specific module isn't installed
Database connection pools exhaust quickly under high load, causing 502s
mitigations: Query the '/admin/modules' endpoint at startup to build a dynamic map of available features
Tune Postgres max_connections and Medusa's internal pool size appropriately

pitfalls: Complex product variants with multiple options are difficult to map via API
Webhooks can duplicate events during store maintenance windows
mitigations: Map variants explicitly by option IDs rather than relying on array ordering
Make all webhook processing idempotent based on the webhook ID

pitfalls: Platform stability has been questionable since recent ownership changes
API documentation frequently lags behind actual API behavior
mitigations: Wrap all Swell calls in extensive error logging to catch undocumented schema changes
Have a migration path ready to Shopify or BigCommerce

pitfalls: Session initialization is slow and can timeout on obscure browser/OS combinations
Local testing tunnels can drop connections if idle for more than a few minutes
mitigations: Stick to the top 5 most popular browser/OS matrix combinations
Implement a keep-alive ping in your local tunnel if running long idle sessions

pitfalls: WebSocket connections to browser instances can drop silently
Limited concurrency on lower tiers leads to queueing delays that look like timeouts
mitigations: Build a reconnection loop for WebSocket browser connections
Pre-check cluster capacity via their API before attempting to spawn sessions

pitfalls: Newer platform, so debugging tools and logs are limited when a session hangs
CAPTCHAs encountered by the browser require manual intervention or third-party solvers
mitigations: Set a hard 60-second timeout on all page.goto commands
Route sessions through a CAPTCHA solving proxy explicitly if targeting protected sites

pitfalls: Selenium's architecture is inherently chatty, causing massive latency over cloud WAN links
Stale element exceptions require complex retry logic that bloats agent code
mitigations: Migrate to Playwright if possible to reduce protocol chatter
Implement an explicit 'wait for element state' wrapper around all DOM interactions

pitfalls: Asserting dynamic content can be flaky if the underlying app changes slightly
API changes frequently as they evolve their platform
mitigations: Use resilient selectors (data-testid) instead of CSS/XPath
Pin your Checkly CLI version in CI/CD pipelines

pitfalls: Instances frequently fail to boot or boot into a corrupted state
Documentation is sparse, leading to a lot of trial-and-error with API parameters
mitigations: Always assert that a basic page load works before handing control to the agent
Have a BrowserBase fallback ready

pitfalls: Sandbox startup times can take 3-5 seconds, ruining fast agent loops
Sandboxes are highly isolated; installing system packages via apt can fail if network is restricted
mitigations: Implement a 'warm pool' of E2B sandboxes that are pre-initialized and kept ready
Pre-bake all required system dependencies into a custom E2B template

pitfalls: Cold starts on custom images can exceed 60 seconds if the image is large
Image registry authentication requires specific Modal CLI setup, breaking pure API usage
mitigations: Keep Modal base images as small as possible (e.g., slim python)
Use pre-built Modal images for common tasks instead of custom Dockerfiles

pitfalls: Shared infrastructure means your code execution can be throttled by noisy neighbors
Outbound network restrictions block many internal/corporate API endpoints
mitigations: Do not use Replit for production agent execution loops
Test outbound connectivity to your specific required endpoints before relying on Replit

pitfalls: RapidAPI wrapper adds an extra layer of latency and rate limiting
Insecure execution of untrusted code can sometimes be exploited to DoS the endpoint
mitigations: Self-host Judge0 CE if you need high-volume or unrestricted execution
Strictly sanitize any user-agent provided code before submitting

pitfalls: 25MB file size limit is easily exceeded by long audio recordings
Silent failures on unsupported audio codecs return empty text strings instead of errors
mitigations: Chunk large audio files locally using ffmpeg before sending to the API
Validate the audio MIME type strictly before uploading

pitfalls: Free tier only allows 60 calls/minute, easily exhausted by a polling agent
Geocoding API is separate and slower, causing 2-step latency for string inputs
mitigations: Aggressively cache weather results (data only changes every 10-15 mins anyway)
Use Lat/Lon coordinates directly instead of city names

pitfalls: Free tier completely blocks production domains (requires localhost)
Frequent source removals break automated scraping logic
mitigations: Pay for the developer tier if using in any deployed environment
Abstract source names to variables so they can be updated when NewsAPI removes them

pitfalls: 5 requests per minute and 500 per day limit is brutally restrictive
Returns 'Note' instead of error JSON when rate limited, breaking standard error parsers
mitigations: Implement a local 15-second sleep between requests
Explicitly check if the root JSON key is 'Note' and handle it as a rate limit error

pitfalls: Quota is consumed heavily by image URLs; adding 'addImage=true' burns through limits fast
Inconsistent data models between search results and detailed recipe endpoints
mitigations: Fetch images only when strictly necessary
Write a normalization layer to map search results to detailed result schemas

pitfalls: Returns a 202 Accepted for asynchronous enrichment, which many HTTP clients interpret as success
Webhook delivery for async results can fail silently
mitigations: If using the async endpoint, implement polling as a fallback for missed webhooks
Treat 202 responses as 'pending', not 'success'

pitfalls: Email verification API is separate and consumes extra credits
Data can be outdated for fast-changing startups
mitigations: Cross-reference Hunter.io results with LinkedIn data if accuracy is critical
Batch domain searches to reduce API overhead

pitfalls: Sandbox environment behaves completely differently than production (different error codes)
Item login tokens expire and require 'Items:update' webhooks to be handled perfectly
mitigations: Implement robust error mapping between Sandbox and Production environments
Alert immediately when an 'LOGIN_REQUIRED' webhook is received

pitfalls: No HTTPS by default on the base domain (must explicitly use api.ipify.org)
Occasional DNS issues on their free tier
mitigations: Hardcode the HTTPS URL in your application
Use a local fallback (like curl ifconfig.me) if ipify fails

pitfalls: Fraud guard can silently block legitimate verification attempts if traffic looks automated
SMS channel is heavily carrier-dependent; app push channel is much more reliable
mitigations: Prefer 'whatsapp' or 'totp' channels over 'sms' for agent-initiated flows
Monitor the 'fraud' webhooks to detect if your agents are triggering Twilio's anti-fraud systems

pitfalls: Workflow run logs are massive and can cause OOM if downloaded uncompressed
Re-running a workflow via API can trigger infinite loops if not gated properly
mitigations: Always stream workflow logs rather than loading them into memory
Pass a unique 'cause' parameter when triggering runs via API to prevent loopbacks

pitfalls: Streaming endpoints can drop chunks if the upstream network blips
Balancing different language models increases latency significantly
mitigations: Implement a local buffering layer for streaming audio before sending to Deepgram
Specify a single language model if you know the input language beforehand

pitfalls: Upload endpoint has a strict file size limit (varies, but usually around 2GB for direct upload)
Leopard (their real-time model) is less accurate than Universal, but agents often pick it for speed
mitigations: Use their 'upload_url' parameter to pass pre-hosted S3 files for large audio
Benchmark accuracy differences for your specific use-case before defaulting to Leopard

pitfalls: High-volume agent loops absolutely flood LangSmith, degrading both the agent and LangSmith
Nested run structures are complex to query effectively via their API
mitigations: Implement dynamic sampling; only send 1/10 runs to LangSmith in production
Use local logging for debugging, LangSmith strictly for performance profiling

pitfalls: Wandb expects a local directory to cache files; fails in restrictive serverless environments
Network isolation breaks the SDK silently, causing data loss without crashing the agent
mitigations: Set WANDB_MODE=disabled if telemetry is not strictly required to save resources
Set WANDB_SILENT=true and WANDB_DIR=/tmp to avoid filesystem permission errors

pitfalls: Acts as a proxy; if Helicone goes down, your LLM calls go down
Custom properties payload size limits can cause silent logging failures
mitigations: Implement a circuit breaker that bypasses the Helicone proxy if it returns 502s
Keep custom properties payloads under 1KB
